answer:
Inner created
Inner created
Outer created
Outer destroyed
Inner destroyed
Inner destroyed
explanation_md: Members are constructed in declaration order before the constructor body runs. Members are destroyed in reverse declaration order after the destructor body runs. So `a` is constructed, then `b`, then the Outer body; on destruction, the Outer body runs first, then `b` is destroyed, then `a`.

prompt_md: Which code snippet correctly expresses composition of a `Point` inside a `Circle`?
explanation_md: Composition means the contained object is stored by value inside the containing object and its lifetime is managed automatically. Option a uses dynamic allocation with a raw pointer (requires manual deletion). Option b is a reference member (not ownership). Option d makes it shared across all instances.

options.a: Logger's constructor is not compatible with App's constructor initializer list.
options.b: App has no destructor to delete log, so Logger is never destroyed and its memory is leaked.
options.c: Logger must be stored by value for its destructor to run.
options.d: new Logger{} calls the wrong constructor overload.

explanation_md: App allocates a Logger with `new` but never `delete`s it. Without a destructor that calls `delete log`, the Logger destructor never runs and the memory is leaked. The fix is to add `~App() { delete log; }`, or better, store Logger by value for true composition.

Members are initialized in their *declaration* order, regardless of the order in the initializer list. `first` is declared before `second`, so `A(1)` is printed first, then `A(2)`, then the B constructor body prints "B".

prompt_md: When should you move from composition (by-value member) to using `std::unique_ptr` for the composed sub-object?

options.a: When the sub-object needs to be shared between multiple containers.
options.b: When the sub-object is large and heap-allocating it avoids large stack frames, but the ownership is still one-to-one.
options.c: When the sub-object's lifetime should outlive the containing object.
options.d: When you need the sub-object to have a reference member.

explanation_md: std::unique_ptr preserves composition semantics (one owner, automatic destruction) while heap-allocating the object. Option a describes shared ownership (std::shared_ptr or aggregation). Option c is aggregation, not composition. Option d is unrelated.
